title: STANDARD FOR LIMES CXS 213-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR LIMES CXS 213-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document title: 'Standard for Limes CXS 213-1999' with adoption in 1999 and amendments in 2005 and 2011.

Defines the scope as commercial varieties of limes from Citrus latifolia Tanaka (Rutaceae) supplied fresh to the consumer after preparation and packaging, excluding limes intended for industrial processing.

Sets quality provisions including detailed minimum requirements (whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests/damage/bruising, no abnormal moisture or foreign smell/taste, firm, free of low-temperature damage, pipless), requires appropriate development/ripeness, establishes a minimum juice content of 42% and colouring requirements (typical on at least two-thirds of surface; up to 30% yellowing allowed), and defines three classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with progressively relaxed allowances for slight defects and specified maximum skin defect areas (1.0 cm2 for Class I allowances, 2.0 cm2 for Class II allowances).

Lists the minimum requirements for all classes: limes must be whole, sound (no rot), clean and practically free of visible foreign matter and pests or pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odour/taste, firm, free of low-temperature damage, practically free of bruising and pipless.

sections.section: 2.1.1 The limes must have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness in accordance with criteria proper to the variety and to the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Requires limes to have reached appropriate development and ripeness per variety and growing area so they can withstand transport and handling and arrive in satisfactory condition at destination.

Specifies minimum juice content calculated by weight as 42% and colouring requirements that the fruit be typically green on at least two-thirds of its surface while allowing up to 30% yellow discolouration.
Defines classification of limes into three classes ('Extra', Class I, Class II) with progressively relaxed quality allowances and refers to class-specific defect tolerances and minimum requirements.

'Extra' Class limes must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation in the package.

Class I limes must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety and may exhibit slight defects in shape, colouring or skin (skin defects ≤ 1.0 cm²) provided these do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality, presentation or the pulp.

Class II covers limes not qualifying for higher classes but meeting minimum requirements and may show defects in shape, colouring or skin (skin defects ≤ 2.0 cm²) provided they retain essential characteristics for quality, keeping quality and presentation and defects do not affect the pulp.

Size is determined by maximum equatorial diameter using five size codes (1:58–67 mm to 5:42–49 mm), limes <42 mm are excluded, packing by count is permitted within two adjacent codes, and specific uniformity rules set maximum size differences for layered, non-layered/rigid and bulk/non-rigid packings (e.g., ≤7 mm within a single code for layered packs).

Allows specified quality and size tolerances per package for produce not meeting the indicated class: class-specific quality tolerances (Extra 5%, Class I 10%, Class II 10% with exceptions) and a size tolerance of 10% by number/weight for fruit one size above or below the declared size, with an absolute minimum diameter of 40 mm.

Specifies quality tolerances by class: 'Extra' allows 5% not meeting the class but meeting Class I (or its tolerances); Class I allows 10% not meeting the class but meeting Class II (or its tolerances); Class II allows 10% failing both the class and minimum requirements except produce rendered unfit for consumption by rotting or other deterioration.
For 'Extra' Class, up to 5% by number or weight of limes in the package may fail to meet 'Extra' requirements provided they meet Class I or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
For Class I, up to 10% by number or weight of limes in the package may fail to meet Class I requirements provided they meet Class II or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

sections.section: 1 It is an acid lime having large fruits called also, depending upon the country: Bearss, Persian, Tahiti.
sections.summary: Informative note identifying the lime as an acid lime with large fruits, also known in some countries as Bearss, Persian or Tahiti (variety/common-name note).

For Class II, up to 10% by number or weight of limes in the package may neither meet the class nor the minimum requirements, except that produce affected by rotting or other deterioration rendering it unfit for consumption is excluded.
Size tolerances allow 10% by number or weight of fruit to be the size immediately above or below the declared size for all classes, and in no case may diameter be less than 40 mm.

Packages must be uniform in origin, variety, quality and size (with 'Extra' requiring uniform colour), and packaging must protect the limes using new, clean, non-damaging materials, comply with CXC 44-1995, and use containers meeting hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements and free of foreign matter or smell.
Requires each package to contain only limes uniform in origin, variety, quality and size, with 'Extra' Class requiring uniform colour and the visible contents representative of the whole.

Packaging must protect the produce; internal materials must be new, clean and non-damaging (labels/printing allowed only with non-toxic ink/glue), and packing must comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Containers must meet specified quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of limes and must be free of foreign matter and odours.
Heading only: introduces marking and labelling provisions but contains no substantive provisions in this section.
Introductory provision stating that consumer package labelling must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and that additional specific provisions follow.
If produce is not visible from outside the consumer package, the package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may indicate the variety.
Non-retail containers must bear, legibly and indelibly on the same side and visible from the outside (or in accompanying documents), the required particulars for the shipment.
Requires the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher on the package label and permits an optional identification code.
Requires the name of the produce on the package when contents are not visible from the outside and allows the variety to be stated optionally.
Requires the country of origin to be declared and optionally the district or national/regional/local place name where grown.

Requires commercial identification including class, size (size code or minimum and maximum diameter in millimetres), optional net weight, and where fruit are packed in layers, the size code (or adjacent codes/min‑max diameters) and number of fruit.
Heading only: indicates an optional official inspection mark but contains no substantive requirements.
Mandates that the produce comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.
Recommends handling and preparation of the produce in accordance with relevant Codex hygiene texts and states the produce should meet any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.

sections.section: 8.1 It is recommended that the produce covered by the provisions of this Standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003), and other relevant Codex texts such as Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.
sections.summary: Recommends that produce be prepared and handled in accordance with the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and other relevant Codex Codes of Practice.

sections.section: 8.2 The produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
sections.summary: States that the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
